diagrams.description: This timeline traces the emergence and evolution of Social Psychology. It begins with Norman Triplett's 1898 social facilitation study, followed by the first textbooks by McDougall and Ross in 1908. Kurt Lewin, the 'father of experimental social psychology', established field theory and action research in the 1930s‑40s. The post‑war period produced landmark experiments: Asch's conformity (1951), Festinger's cognitive dissonance (1957), and Milgram's obedience studies (1963). The 1970s‑80s saw the Stanford prison experiment (1971), Tajfel's social identity theory, and the rise of social cognition and attribution research. The 1990s brought evolutionary social psychology and implicit measures (IAT, 1998). Contemporary social psychology integrates neuroscience, cross‑cultural perspectives, and replication reforms.
diagrams.mermaid_code:
timeline title History of Social Psychology section Early Roots 1898 : Norman Triplett
Social facilitation study 1908 : First textbooks
McDougall & Ross section Foundational Era 1930s-40s : Kurt Lewin
Field theory & action research 1951 : Solomon Asch
Conformity experiments section Golden Age 1957 : Leon Festinger
Cognitive dissonance theory 1963 : Stanley Milgram
Obedience to authority 1971 : Philip Zimbardo
Stanford prison experiment section Cognitive & Social Identity 1970s : Henri Tajfel
Social identity theory 1980s : Attribution & social cognition
(Heider, Kelley, Fiske) section Modern Developments 1990s : Evolutionary social psychology
Cultural psychology 1998 : Implicit Association Test
(Greenwald & Banaji) 2000s : Social neuroscience
fMRI, TMS 2010s : Replication crisis &
open science reforms
